coredata.dc:title: Ultrathin layered MoS2 and N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) anchored reduced graphene oxide (rGO) nanocomposite-based counter electrode for dye-sensitized solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Silambarasan, K.
Harish, S.
Hara, K.
Archana, J.
Navaneethan, M.
coredata.dc:description: Numerous inorganic and organic counter electrodes (CEs) have been fabricated for dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) instead of platinum (Pt) CE. However, MoS2 and carbon nanocomposite have played an important role in CEs due to their superior electrochemical properties and high chemical stability. N-doped graphene quantum dot (N-GQD) @ MoS2 @ reduced graphene oxide (rGO) nanocomposite was synthesized by the two-step hydrothermal method. The morphology of as-synthesized nanocomposites was studied using field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) and scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM). It confirms the formation of sphere-like MoS2 composed of nanosheets on the surface of rGO sheets. The N-GQD@MoS2@rGO composites confirmed the presence of MoS2, rGO, and N-GQD by X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Raman spectra. The chemical composition and purity of N-GQD@MoS2@rGO was examined by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis technique. The electrochemical property of the as-fabricated CEs was studied by cyclic voltammetry (CV) analysis by using the iodine-based electrolyte. The N-GQD@MoS2@rGO shows the superior catalytic property due to more electrochemical active site and electrical conductivity property of rGO and MoS2. The DSSCs device assembled with as-fabricated CEs and their photovoltaic power conversion efficiency (η) of MoS2 was 2.01%, MoS2@rGO was 3.92%, N-GQD@MoS2 was 3.53%, N-GQD@MoS2@rGO was 4.65%, and Pt was 5.17%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: DSSCs
MoS2
Cyclic voltammetry
Photovoltaic
Graphene quantum dot

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of counter electrode for DSSCs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of synthesis method. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
TEM and HR-TEM image: (a & b) MoS2, (c & d) MoS2@rGO, and (e & f) N-GQD@MoS2.

(a) FE-SEM image of N-GQD@MoS2@rGO, (b & d) TEM and HR-TEM image of N-GQD@MoS2@rGO, (d–h) STEM elemental distribution mapping of N-GQD@MoS2@rGO, and (i) EDAX spectrum image of N-GQD@MoS2@rGO. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of N-GQD. Inset is the photographs of the N-GQD under visible light and UV lamp, respectively. (b) XRD patterns of MoS2, MoS2@rGO, N-GQD@MoS2, and N-GQD@MoS2@rGO. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Raman spectrum of MoS2, MoS2@rGO, N-GQD@MoS2, and N-GQD@MoS2@rGO. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XPS analysis of N-GQD@MoS2@rGO, (a) Survey spectrum and core level spectrum, (b) O 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) C 1s, (e) Mo 3d, and (f) S 2p. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) CV analysis of MoS2, MoS2@rGO, N-GQD@MoS2, and N-GQD@MoS2@rGO, and Pt, (b) Different scan rate of N-GQD@MoS2@rGO, (c) J-V curves of DSSCs based on MoS2, MoS2@rGO, N-GQD@MoS2, and N-GQD@MoS2@rGO, and Pt CEs, and (d) current density of DSSCs, and redox couple vs different CEs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) Adsorption-desorption isotherms of MoS2@rGO, and N-GQD@MoS2@rGO, and (b) Nyquist plots of the symmetrical MoS2@rGO, and N-GQD@MoS2@rGO. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Photovoltaic and electrochemical properties of as fabricated CEs.
BET and electrochemical parameters of MoS2@rGO, and N-GQD@MoS2@rGO.

head.ce:title: Ultrathin layered MoS2 and N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) anchored reduced graphene oxide (rGO) nanocomposite-based counter electrode for dye-sensitized solar cells

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Functional Materials and Energy Devices Laboratory
SRM Institute of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Numerous inorganic and organic counter electrodes (CEs) have been fabricated for dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) instead of platinum (Pt) CE. However, MoS2 and carbon nanocomposite have played an important role in CEs due to their superior electrochemical properties and high chemical stability. N-doped graphene quantum dot (N-GQD) @ MoS2 @ reduced graphene oxide (rGO) nanocomposite was synthesized by the two-step hydrothermal method. The morphology of as-synthesized nanocomposites was studied using field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) and scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM). It confirms the formation of sphere-like MoS2 composed of nanosheets on the surface of rGO sheets. The N-GQD@MoS2@rGO composites confirmed the presence of MoS2, rGO, and N-GQD by X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Raman spectra. The chemical composition and purity of N-GQD@MoS2@rGO was examined by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis technique. The electrochemical property of the as-fabricated CEs was studied by cyclic voltammetry (CV) analysis by using the iodine-based electrolyte. The N-GQD@MoS2@rGO shows the superior catalytic property due to more electrochemical active site and electrical conductivity property of rGO and MoS2. The DSSCs device assembled with as-fabricated CEs and their photovoltaic power conversion efficiency (η) of MoS2 was 2.01%, MoS2@rGO was 3.92%, N-GQD@MoS2 was 3.53%, N-GQD@MoS2@rGO was 4.65%, and Pt was 5.17%.

schematic diagram of DSSCs fabrication with N-GQD@MoS2@rGO CEs.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: The remarkable increase in the need for energy sources has motivated the researchers to design and fabricate cost-effective devices. The dye-sensitized solar cell (DSSC) has been a prominent energy production device due to its easy processing, cost-effectiveness and has easy fabrication methods compare to other conventional solar cells. DSSC has achieved a certified power conversion efficiency of 14.3%, which is equal to that of commercialized polycrystalline-silicon (14%) solar cell [1–5]. Nevertheless, the power conversion of DSSC largely depends on the appropriate selection of CE materials. Till date, platinum (Pt) based catalysts were widely used for the reduction of electrolyte in DSSC, since it has excellent electro-catalytic activity [6–8]. The cost and scarcity of Pt were not appropriate for the large-scale production of DSSCs which leads to an increase in the cost of DSSC fabrication [9–11]. Different kinds of materials were investigated to replace the Pt such as organic polymers [12], transition metal dichalcogenides [13], oxide-based materials [14,15], nitrides-based materials [16,17], carbon-based materials [18,19], among which the transition metal dichalcogenides materials show excellent power conversion efficiency when compared to other inorganic materials, due to their superior electrochemical property.

The 2D layered transition metal dichalcogenide (MoS2, WS2, and MoSe2) was considered as a future CEs in the DSSCs due to its advantageous properties such as cost, abundant nature, and high electrocatalytic activity and chemical stability. Among the above-mentioned transition metal dichalcogenide (TMD) materials, layered MoS2 has exhibited higher catalytic activity and efficiency when compared with other TMDs materials. It is reported that in the structure of MoS2 material the occurrence of Sulphur (S) atoms was large in numbers at the edge layers [it is reported that in the structure of MoS2, the Sulphur (S) atoms were in large numbers at the edges of the layers and it was further verified by experimental and theoretical studies.]. It is proved in experimental and theoretical studies [20–23]. These S atoms easily adsorb the redox ions in the electrolyte and boost up the reduction process. In MoS2 layered structure, the Mo and S atoms were arranged with covalent bonding in each layer sandwiched as S–Mo–S. Whereas, these layers were attached one to another by van der Waals forces. Several synthesis methods have been investigated for different structural and morphology of MoS2 CEs in Pt free DSSCs. Infant Raj et al. had synthesized the vertically aligned MoS2 sheets as CEs by the chemical vapor deposition (CVD) and achieved an efficiency of η = 7.50%; In which the vertical aligned MoS2 CEs are the reason to obtained maximum power conversion by the internal reflection of light in the vertical MoS2 [24]. M. Al-Mamun et al. had optimized the ratio of the source materials as 1:28 also varied the reaction temperature and time. They fabricated the device using the optimized material as CEs and achieved the η of 7.41% [25]. B. Lei and co-workers were examined the different morphology of MoS2 CEs for Pt free CEs in the DSSC application. And they have reported the higher power conversion efficiency of η = 5.41 for MoS2 nanoparticles CE compared to for a few-layer MoS2 CE (1.74%) and multilayer MoS2 CE (2.92%) and it was due to the highest electrocatalytic activity [26]. J. Liang et al. studied the effect of the hydrothermal growth over a period in MoS2 CEs (directly grown ultrathin MoS2 nanofilms on FTO) for DSSCs application. The photovoltaic power conversion efficiency of DSSCs was increased with increasing the growth period, in which the 15 h growth MoS2 nanofilms show the maximum value (η = 8.3%) when compared to 5 and 10 h growth MoS2 nanofilms. it is due to increased coverage area of MoS2 on FTO surface [27].

From the literature, it is clear that MoS2 based CEs materials shows excellent electrochemical and power conversion property, even though electrical conductivity of the MoS2 is low. The electrical conductivity of the CEs materials is also one of the key factors for boosting the power conversion efficiency of the DSSCs devices. Different methods were adopted to increase the electrical conductivity of MoS2 such as doping with metal atoms, composite with carbon-based and other compounds. The two-dimensional (2D) graphene materials were formed conjectured sp2 (G band) hybridization carbon atom, where these basal planes lead to high electrical conductivity. Since, the basal plane of graphene has low electrochemical properties, it leads to the limited number of active surface sites to I3 reduction on the surface of the graphene [28–31]. Thus, the electrical conductivity of the MoS2 can be increased when it is composite with carbon materials. On the other hand, this kind of 2D carbon materials acts as a platform to reduce the agglomeration of MoS2 and enhances the electrochemical active spots. C. Yu et al. had studied the counter electrode property of MoS2 with graphene-mediated by two-step CVD processes and they obtained a maximum power conversion efficiency of 7.1% [32]. G. Yue et al. had synthesized MoS2@graphene by ball milling method, where multi-layer graphene powder and MoS2 powder were used as source materials. They optimized the thickness of the sample by varying the weight % of graphene in the graphene MoS2 composite. Thus, they achieved the η = 5.98% for the 6 μm thickness sample [33]. However, the electrochemical property of MoS2 was not high.

N-doped graphene quantum dot (N-GQD) is zero-dimensional graphene sheets, where it contains the π-π conjugated bonds and various oxygen functional groups. The orientation of GQDs alter with nitrogen (N) which gives more active sites in the zero-dimensional materials. Several groups reported the catalytic property of the quantum dots were high due to their intrinsic property [34]. When the N-GQDs was supported on materials, it shows better electrochemical property compared with unsupported N-GQDs. So, in this work, we have synthesized N-GQD@MoS2@rGO nanocomposite by two-step hydrothermal method. Further, the samples were characterized using XPS, Raman, XRD, FESEM and HRTEM analysis. The synthesized materials were used to fabrication CE thin film and their catalytic property was studied using cyclic voltammetry analysis. The photovoltaic device was fabricated using as-fabricated CE thin film and studied their performance. For comparison, Pt CEs performance also studied, and their result were compared with other CEs.

Hexaammonium heptamolybdate tetrahydrate [(NH4)6Mo7O24.4H2O], thioacetamide [C2H5NS], citric acid [C6H8O7], ethylenediamine [C2H8N2], sulfuric acid [H2SO4] and graphite powder were purchased from Wako Chemicals, Japan. All chemicals were used without further purification (purification of all the chemicals are ≥ 99.0%).

N-GQD was synthesized by hydrothermal method, 1.68 g of citric acid was added in 40 mL of DI water and stirred for some time, and later 0.6 M (1.44 g) of ethylenediamine was added to the above solution gradually and allowed to stirrer 1 h (Here, citric acid has used as carbon source material for quantum dots, and ethylenediamine plays a dual role such as; source material for N doping and capping agent). Then, the clear solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and kept in the electric oven at 160 °C for 4 h. The graphene oxide was synthesized from graphite powder by chemical oxidation method. In the chemical reduction process, epoxides, alcohols, ketones, and carboxylic groups were introduced in natural graphite powder under the strong acid medium (modified Hummer's method) [35,36].

The N-GQD@MoS2@rGO hybrid was synthesized by hydrothermal method. Initially, 0.002 mM (106 mg) of ammonium molybdate tetrahydrate was added 40 mL of DI water, then stirred for 15 min. Then, 0.08 mM (240 mg) of thioacetamide was slowly added on the above homogeneous solution and stirred for a 30 min. 40 mL of GO solution (1 mg of GO per mL) poured in the above solution. After 15 min of stirring, 1 mL of as-synthesized N-GQD added and stirred for 10 min. Then the mixture was transferred into Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 24 h in an electric oven. The black precipitate was collected and washed several times with DI water and ethanol. Finally, the product was dried at 80 °C for overnight and annealed at 400 °C for 3 h under Argon atmosphere. The resultant product named as N-GQD@MoS2@rGO. For comparison MoS2, MoS2@rGO, and N-GQD@MoS2 were also synthesized under the same reaction condition.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured by UV–Vis spectrometer (JASCO V-750). Crystalline property of the as-synthesized nanocomposites was studied by using an X-ray diffractometer (RINT-2200, Rigaku) with Cu Kα radiation. Morphology and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) of as-synthesized nanocomposite was investigating a scanning electron microscope (SEM: JEOL JSM 7001F) with an acceleration voltage of 15 kV and Scanning Transmission Electron Microscopy (STEM JEOL-2100; accelerating voltage of 200 k. Raman spectra were measured by Microscopy Raman spectrometer (NRS-7100, JASCO). Elemental analysis of as-synthesized materials was measured by a photoelectron spectrometer (ESCA 3100, Shimadzu). The electrochemical activity and stability of as-fabricated thin-films were characterized by three electrode electrochemical workstation (1280C, Solartron Analytical). The current-voltage (I–V) characteristics of the fabricated photovoltaic devices were studied by a Class A solar simulator workstation (SL-50A-WS, SCIENCETECH) at an air mass of AM1.5 (sunlight of 100 mW cm−2). Surface area of the as-synthesized samples were characterized by Quantachrome Instruments, Autosorb IQ series.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of counter electrode for DSSCs
ce:section.ce:para: The schematic diagram of counter electrode was shown in Fig. S1. Typically, 0.8 g of N-GQD@MoS2@rGO was dispersed in 5 mL of ethanol and ground well for 45 min using mortar and pestle. The homogeneous solution was transfer into beaker and then the solution was made up to 100 mL using addition of ethanol. The 50 μL of triton-X (organic binder) was added into above solution and ultrasonicated for 30 min. The above mixture solution was spray coated on fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) substrate at 120 °C. Finally, the N-GQD@MoS2@rGO coated film was annealed at 300 °C for 4 h in an Ar atmosphere. Same fabrication method followed to prepare all other counter electrode. The thickness of fabricated counter electrode was measured by using cross-section image of FE-SEM. The cross-sectional image of as-fabricated counter electrode shown in Fig. S2. It clearly shows uniform film coated on FTO substrate around 8–9 μm thickness.

Redox property of as-fabricated CEs was characterized by CV analysis. Pt-coated FTO redox also studied comparative analysis with the fabricated CEs. The CV analysis carried out in the three-electrode system; the reference electrode is Ag/AgCl, the counter electrode is Pt foil, and CEs are as-fabricated CEs and Pt-coated FTO CE. This three-electrode placed on iodine-based electrolyte. It consists of I2 (12.6 mg), LiI (66.9 mg), and LiClO4 (531.9 mg) in the 50 mL of acetonitrile.

The photoanode was fabricated by following process, Titanium dioxide (TiO2) (commercial P25) was dispersed in 100 mL ethanol, and then 50 μL of triton-X (binder) added the above solution. The mixture of solution was sonicated by 30 min and coated on fluorine doped tin oxide (FTO) substrate by using a spray coating technique. Finally, the photoanode coated FTO was annealed at 450 °C for 2 h and the thickness of the photoanode was ⁓ 16.5 μm (Fig. S3). After that photoanode was dipped in a sensitizer di-tetrabutylammonium cis-bis (isothiocyanate) bis (2,2″- bipyridyl-4,4′dicarboxylato) ruthenium II (N719) solution for 14 h. The sensitizer absorbed photoanode and counter electrode sealed using binding clips. After that commercial iodine-based electrolyte filled into the cell and I–V characterization of the device was performed.

Fig. 1 shows the schematic diagram of the hydrothermal synthesis of N-GQD@MoS2@rGO hybrid. In the initial step; N-GQD was synthesized by the hydrothermal method, where 1.68 g of citric acid and 1.44 g of ethylenediamine were the source materials for carbon and nitrogen. During the hydrothermal process, citric acid was decomposed into carbon, and the nucleation of carbon atom growth-restricted in zero-dimensional by ethylenediamine; here ethylenediamine acts as a nitrogen source material as well as capping agent. The N-GQD@MoS2@rGO was synthesized by the second step hydrothermal method, where GO,as-synthesized N-GQD and source for MoS2 were mixed and kept in an electrical oven. During the second step hydrothermal method, hexaammonium heptamolybdate tetrahydrate, and thioacetamide were decomposed simultaneously and the reaction mechanism is formulated in equations (1) and (2). Meantime, the +6 ionic state of MoO3 was reduced to +4 state at high temperature and further reacts with H2S to formed MoS2 sheets [37,38]. The chemical reaction of MoS2 has given in equations (3) and (4).
(1)
(
NH 4
)
6
MoO 7
· 4 H
2
O →
6 NH
3
+
7 MoO
3
+
3 H
2
O
(2)
C 2
H 5
NS +
2
H 2
O →
NH 3
+
H 2
S +
CO 2
(3)
MoO 3
+
3 H
2
S +
H 2
O →
MoO 2
+ S
O 4
2 -
+
H +
(4)
MoO 2
+
2 H
2
S
→
MoS 2
+
H 2
O

In the modified Hummer's method, carboxylic, hydroxyl, and ketonic functional groups were introduced in the graphite lattice edges and basal plans under strong acid using the oxidation process. During the hydrothermal reaction, carboxylic, hydroxyl, and ketonic functional group of GO were decreased and it has reduced from GO to rGO. Here, most of the functional groups were removed but still, some functional groups had remained at the surface of GO. These functional groups will interact with metal atoms or other functional groups [39]. In the MoS2 and rGO composite, MoS2 and rGO bonded with the weak van der Waal's interaction because both are 2D layered materials [40].

The different factors such as morphology, conductivity and surface area play a significant role to enhance the electrocatalytic reaction. Among them, the morphology analysis was the major key to increase electrochemical active sites (high surface area) during the redox reactions. The surface morphology of the samples was investigated by FE-SEM, TEM, and STEM analysis and it as shown in Figs. 2 and 3
. Fig. 2 (a) shows the TEM image of pure MoS2, which shows the 2D sheet-like structure. In HR-TEM image (Fig. 2 (b)), the lattice fringes of the MoS2 was clearly seen, which indicates the good crystalline nature. The interesting changes happen in the morphology of MoS2 when the carbon materials were added in the hydrothermal method, where carbon materials served as the template to form 3D sphere-like MoS2, which could increase the electrochemical active surface area of the nanocomposite [41,42]. Morphology of MoS2 is notably changed, when GO and N-GQD was added into hydrothermal reaction solution. This kind MoS2@rGO composite (Fig. 2 (c & d)), the small MoS2 sheets were self-assembled and formed sphere-like morphology, where these spheres were attached on the surface of the rGO sheets, where the size of MoS2 was ⁓ 270 nm. The lattice fringes of the MoS2 were clearly seen on the surface of the rGO sheets. From these results, the MoS2 sphere on the surface of rGO was confirmed. The TEM and HR-TEM images of N-GQD@MoS2 were shown in Fig. 2 (e & f), where it shows the sphere-like morphology. However, the size of N-GQD@MoS2 (⁓ 630 nm) was high when compared with MoS2@rGO (⁓ 270 nm). N-doped quantum dots presences was confirmed using STEM analysis and as shown Fig. S4. In the elemental distribution mapping, the Mo and S are distribution on the sphere-like morphology. The C and N atoms also evenly distributed on the sphere structures. From this analysis, the several MoS2 nanosheets was formed a sphere like morphology and N-doped quantum dots are supported on the MoS2. In Fig. S4 (f) shows energy dispersive spectrum of N-GQD@MoS2 sample. No additional peak observed in the spectrum which confirmed there are no impurities in N-GQD@MoS2 samples. For further confirmation, morphology of N-GQD@MoS2@rGO was studied using FE-SEM, TEM, and STEM analysis and shown in Fig. 3. The several MoS2 nanosheets were united and formed sphere-like morphology and it was supported on surface of the rGO sheets (Fig. 3 (a)) (inset image: enlarge image of MoS2 spheres on the rGO sheets). This studied also confirmed the presence of MoS2 nanosheets were joined and formed sphere-like structure (sphere diameter: ⁓ 210–250 nm) and it is anchored on the graphene sheets. The HR-TEM image of N-GQD@MoS2@rGO was shown in Fig. 3 (c). The HR-TEM image shows the clear lattice fringes of MoS2, which was indicated the good crystalline of N-GQD@MoS2@rGO. The elemental distribution mapping and energy dispersive spectrum of N-GQD@MoS2@rGO were characterized by STEM analysis and as shown in in Fig. 3 (d – i). From mapping analysis, Mo and S atoms have uniformly distributed in the sphere like structure on rGO sheets. The N and C atoms distributed over the layered hybrid structures and it confirmed the presence of N-doped quantum dots on the hybrid. The presence of Mo, S, N, and C was confirmed in N-GQD@MoS2@rGO hybrid in the energy dispersive X-ray spectrum (Fig. 3 (i)). No other additional peaks were detected in the EDAX spectrum, which confirmed the formation of N-GQD@MoS2G hybrid without any impurity. From the morphology analysis, the agglomeration of MoS2 nanosheets has reduced while composite with carbon atom. Here, the rGO plays curial role to avoid agglomeration of MoS2 which leads to increasing electrical conductivity of the MoS2. This 3D MoS2 flower-like structure interconnected interstitial carbon atom guaranteed fast mass electron transport for the electrolyte.

The absorption property of N-GQDs was studied using UV–Vis spectroscopy and shown in Fig. 4 (a). The absorption peaks at ⁓ 466 nm was attributed to n -
π ∗
transitions conjugated carbon and nitrogen bonds (CN) also, three other peaks was absorbed at 390 nm, 352 nm, and 282 nm. The absorption peak at 390 nm assigned to n -
π ∗
electron transition of CO, and NO bonds. The two-electron transition in sp2 hybridization of carbon clusters in the N-GQDs. The strong electron affinity interaction (
π ∗
−
π ∗
) of CN was observed at 282 nm. The absorption peaks were confirmed the formation of N-GQDs and strong electron transition property of C, and N bonding were confirmed the existences of N atom in the quantum dots. The N-GQDs solution emits a strong green luminescent (inset of Fig. 2a) under the UV lamp irradiation, which indicates luminescent of N-GQD [43,44]. The crystalline property of as-synthesized hybrid materials was investigated by XRD analysis as shown in Fig. 4b. The diffraction peak at 13.82°, 33.30°, 39.28°, and 59.04° corresponding to the (002), (100), (103), and (110) plane. All the peaks well matched with hexagonal structure of MoS2 (JCPDS card No. 24–0513) [45]. In addition, one board peak was observed at 26.4°, which corresponds to (002) plane of carbon. For comparison, the XRD pattern of GO and rGO were studied and shown in Fig. S2. A sharp peak at 11.58° assigned to (001) plane of GO. XRD pattern of hydrothermally reduced GO (Fig. S5) was showed a wide peak at 24.42° which corresponds to (002) plane of a carbon atom [46–48]. This wide diffraction peak indicates the poor ordering of the sheets along the basal plane direction and many disorders in the carbon lattice. From the XRD analysis, MoS2@carbon composites and the reduction of GO were confirmed. Raman spectroscopy was powerful technique to study the 2D structure materials and their interaction property. In Fig. 5 , The peak at 1337 and 1582 cm−1 attributed to D and G band of rGO, where D band was several disorders in sp2 hybridized carbon sheets and G band was graphitic carbon sp2 of carbon atoms (C–C bond). In addition, we observed two more vibration peaks at lower wavenumber side (376 and 401 cm−1) which corresponds in-plane (
E
2 g
1
)
and out-plane (
A
1 g
) vibration of MoS2 [45,49]. These
E
2 g
1
and
A
1 g
vibration of MoS2 was significant evidence to study the MoS2 and carbon composites. In MoS2 and carbon composites,
E
2 g
1
and
A
1 g
were shifted red and blue shifted when compared to MoS2 which indicates MoS2 interacted with carbon materials with van der Waals interaction. The Raman spectra of graphene oxide was shown in Fig. S6. and the D and G of GO was shown at ⁓1350 and 1596 cm−1. The ratio of the peak intensity of D and G band (IG/ID) of GO is 0.999, MoS2@rGO is 1.031, and N-GQD@MoS2@rGO is 1.008. The IG/ID ratio of the nanocomposite rGO materials was high when compare with GO, this is due to the several disorders in the G band (sp2 hybridization) during the conversion of GO into rGO. The G band of MoS2@rGO and N-GQD@MoS2@rGO has the shift towards (⁓ 13 cm−1) a low wavenumber side when compared with the G band of GO; which was due to the reduction of GO or interaction between the two 2D structure materials (rGO and MoS2 were bonded with weak van der Waals) [40,46–48,50]. From the XRD and Raman analysis, metal sulfide and carbon nanocomposite were confirmed, and these materials were bonded together with weak van der Waals bonding.

The chemical composition and oxidation state of elemental were exanimated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. Fig. 6 a shows the survey spectrum of N-GQD@MoS2@rGO. The presence of O 1s, N 1s, C 1s, Mo 3d, and S 2p was in the hybrid from the survey spectrum. There are no additional peaks in the survey spectrum, which indicates no more impurities in the hybrid. The core level deconvoluted spectrum of O 1s, N 1s, C 1s, Mo 3d, and S 2p are shown in Fig. 6 b-f. The high-resolution spectrum of O 1s (Fig. 6b) displays three peaks at 533.7, 532.5, and 531.3 eV, which attributed to –OH, C–OH, and C–O bonds, respectively. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 6c) showed three peaks at 400.5, 399.5, and 398.8 eV corresponds to graphitic-N, pyrrolic-N, and pyridinic-N, respectively and another predominant peak was observed at ⁓ 394 eV corresponds to Mo 3p. Fig. 6d shows the high-resolution spectrum of C 1s, which confirmed the C–N (286.3 eV) bonding. Some other peaks also observed such as: 284.4, 285.6, and 288.7 eV attributed to CC, C–O, and O–CO bonds, respectively. The core level XPS spectrum of Mo 3d (Fig. 6e) showed two major peaks at 231.8 and 228.5 eV assigned to Mo 3d3/2 and Mo 3d5/2. The core level spectrum of S 2p in Fig. 6f shows two major peaks at 163.2 and 161.9 eV, which corresponds to the S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 orbitals [34]. From the XPS analysis, N 1s presence has confirmed and bonded with carbon atoms. The XPS data were well-matched with UV–Vis and elemental mapping analysis.

The catalytic property and cycling stability of the counter electrode were studied by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and shown in Fig. 7 . Fig. 7a shows the CV results of Pt, MoS2, N-GQD@MoS2, MoS2@rGO, N-GQD@MoS2@rGO, and it was carried out at a scan rate of 50 mV/s−1. In the CV curves, one pair of redox was observed due to the oxidation and reduction of iodine redox pieces, and their physical formula for the chemical reaction was given in equations (5) and (6) [39,49,51].
(5)
3
I −
→
I 3 −
+
2
e −
(6)
I 3 −
+
2
e −
→ 3
I −

The peak to peak separation (Epp) for MOS2, N-GQD@MoS2, MoS2@rGO, N-GQD@MoS2@rGO, and commercial Pt are 473, 460, 414, 385, and 310 mV, respectively. The N-GQD@MoS2@rGO were exhibits high catalytic performance compared with other and Pt due to high electrical conductivity of graphene and more electro chemical activity of MoS2. The CV profile of the MoS2 electrode at different sweep rates ranging from 10 to 80 mV s−1 in the operation voltage of −0.2 to 0.5 V are shown in Fig. 6 b. A pair of strong redox peaks can clearly be seen at −0.1 to 0.37 V (scan rate ∼ 10 mV s−1) because of the high synergistic interaction in MoS2. With an increase in the scan rate from 10 to 80 mV s−1, the redox peaks was shifting towards positive as well as negative potentials, this was well retained with their original shapes of CV curves without any distortion. The current density of N-GQD@MoS2@rGO exhibited high current value when compared with other electrodes. From this analysis, the electrochemical property of N-GQD@MoS2@rGO was enhanced due to a greater number of active sites in the N-doped quantum dots.

The photovoltaic property was studied using MoS2, N-GQD@MoS2, MoS2@rGO, N-GQD@MoS2@rGO, and Pt as CE, where N719 as dye and commercial TiO2 (P25) as a photoanode. The profile of the J-V measurement was shown in Fig. 7 c and their corresponding photovoltaic parameters are shown in Table 1 . The power conversion efficiency (η) of MoS2 is 2.01%, η of MoS2@rGO is 3.92%, η of N-GQD@MoS2 is 3.53%, η of N-GQD@MoS2@rGO is 4.65%, and η of Pt is 5.17%, respectively. The short circuit current density (Jsc) of the as-fabricated DSSCs with N-GQD@MoS2@rGO CE exhibited the highest short circuit current density (8.62 mA cm−2) when compared with MoS2 (4.52 mA cm−2), MoS2@rGO (7.80 mA cm−2) and N-GQD@MoS2 (6.83 mA cm−2) due to the electrical conductivty changes. In Fig. 7 (d) shows the photovoltaic current density and redox current density of different CEs. From the current density comparison, N-GQD@MoS2@rGO nanocomposites CEs show high value when compared to other MoS2 CEs which clearly indicates the electrical conductivity and electrochemical active site of MoS2 were increased by compositing with carbon-based materials. The electrochemical active sites of N-GQD@MoS2@rGO samples showed high power conversion and electrochemical properties when compared with other samples, which is due to the presence of N-doped graphene quantum dots. The graphene allotrope materials have gained much attention in the electrochemical reaction because of their unique electrical and electrochemical properties. The electrical property and electrochemical active sites of the graphene allotrope were increased when the dopant or defects introduce in the carbon matrix. When Nitrogen atoms are doped at edges and near edges (π bonding promote the electron transport) of the carbon matrix which can increase the majority (n-type) charge transportation in the quantum dots. This n-type carrier locally induces the catalytic reaction towards the reduction process [52–56]. Due to that, when the N-GQDs decorated on the surface of the materials has shown better electrochemical activity in iodine-based electrolyte solution. The electrical conductivity, catalytic activity, and current density of the counter electrode materials are inter-dependent property. The carbon-based materials (rGO) in the MoS2 and carbon composites have two functions: the enhancement of the active sites leads to dispersing the electrochemical activity of MoS2 and matrix material for fast charge transfer. This relation was well matched with electrochemical and photovoltaic results. In order to further confirm, we have studied the surface area (Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy as shown in Fig. 8 . In Fig. 8 a shows the adsorption and desorption property of MoS2@rGO, and N-GQD@MoS2@rGO, which exhibit a (IV) isotherm patterns. Surface area of the nanocomposites were calculated from BET results and tabulated in Table 3
. From BET results, when incorporated N-GQD in the MoS2@rGO can enhanced the active sites of the CE.

The electron transport property of the MoS2@rGO, and N-GQD@MoS2@rGO was studied by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Fig. 7b shows Nyquist plots of the symmetric cells with different CEs and relevant equivalent circuit of respective cells. The electrical component of each electrode was studied using equivalent circuit and parameters as tabulated in Table 2. The charge transport resistance of the MoS2@rGO, and N-GQD@MoS2@rGO are 30.4 and 13 Ω, respectively. The N-GQD@MoS2@rGO shows low charge transfer resistance value when compared with MoS2@rGO, which clearly show N-GQD@MoS2@rGO has more electrical and electrochemical property. The BET and EIS results were well matched with electrochemical and photovoltaic results. N-GQDs are improve the surface area and electrical property of the CEs.

The commercial Pt CE shows high electrochemical property and photovoltaic performance with other CE due to the high electrical conductivity and electrochemical property of Pt. The photovoltaic results were well-matched with CV analysis. However, the cost of Pt is too high when compared with Mo. The MoS2 with carbon composite materials are a better choice to replace the Pt CE in DSSCs application. The efficiency of some MoS2/carbon composities was tabulated in Table 3 and compared with our results. The power conversiton efficiency of the N-GQD@MoS2@rGO CE sample show littile low compared with other MoS2/carbon CEs which may be photoanode (TiO2 double layer) of the DSSCs devies. Here double layer means, they used scattering and measo-poures layer. However, our system has only TiO2(P25) nanoparticles. If we go for double layer photoanode, the efficiency of the device can enfance.

N-GQD@MoS2@rGO and other samples were synthesized by hydrothermal method. Sphere-like MoS2 nanosheets was decorated on the surface of rGO without any aggregation in MoS2@rGO, and N-GQD@MoS2@rGO. STEM EDX mapping confirmed the presents of Mo, S, N, and C in the composite, and Mo and S were shown sphere structure, where it has confirmed the MoS2 sphere-like morphology. XRD diffraction pattern confirmed the hexagonal phase of MoS2 has present in all diffraction patterns. Raman spectra confirmed the reduction of GO into rGO during the hydrothermal reaction. XPS analysis has confirmed the presence of N-GQD in the composite. N-GQD@MoS2@rGO showed the lowermost Epp value when compared with other CEs, which shows higher power conversion efficiency (4.65%) due to high catalytic. The results illustrated that N-GQD@MoS2@rGO composite are potential candidates to replace Pt in DSSCs.

K. Silambarasan: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing—Original Draft. S. Harish: Investigation, Data Curation, Writing—Original Draft. K. Hara: Visualization, Writing—Review & Editing. J. Archana: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. M. Navaneethan: Idea, Investigation, Visualization, Writing—Original Draft.

The author acknowledges the Center for Nanodevices fabrication, Shizuoka University, Hamamatsu, Japan for support of the instrument facilities.
